key_insight: Sulfur vacancies in MoS2 do not diffuse independently; instead, they form concentration-dependent clusters that can transition from immobile defects to connected networks and finally to classical diffusion.
engineering_relevance: Because vacancy mobility changes strongly with defect concentration, MoS2 device behavior such as memristive switching can be tuned or destabilized by local defect clustering.
method: Kinetic Monte Carlo simulations of sulfur-vacancy motion in MoS2
Transition rates derived from molecular dynamics with a machine-learning interatomic potential
Cluster statistics used to identify transport regimes across vacancy concentrations
evidence_signal: The abstract reports three distinct regimes, including a broad intermediate cluster-size distribution and a concentration-dependent diffusion coefficient, supporting the link between defect density and transport.
watch_item: The diffusion behavior is inferred from simulations and rate models, so device-scale predictions may depend on the accuracy of the learned potential and the assumed vacancy environment.
paper_title: Self-organized defect clustering and concentration-dependent vacancy diffusion in MoS$_2$
